channel.title: Proposals And Policy (new posts)
channel.description: Posts in the forum category "Proposals And Policy" - What can we do to improve the site? Ask any questions you may have regarding site structure and policy.

item.title: What about a Canadien Site?: Re: What about a Canadien Site?
item.content:encoded:
The sites are based on language, not nationality. English-speaking Canadian users would be most at home on -EN, while primarily-French-speaking Canadians would likely be more comfortable on -FR. There is absolutely no reason to institute a "Canadian Branch" when the major official languages1 are covered already.

There is nothing stopping someone from writing Canada-based content here, on -FR, or, indeed, on literally any of the wikis.

Footnotes
1. Can't speak for the First Nations' languages. If someone fluent wants to create a site for those, that'd be neat.

item.title: What about a Canadien Site?: What about a Canadien Site?
item.content:encoded: Hey! So I have seen that there's a Site for (almost) all languages, and I wonder if a country such as Canada or the United Kingdom could have their own Site or a type of SCP/Goi/Canon for them. I know that the other Sites are basically just translation, but it could make people from America wanna see Goi Canadien and England, and after that, they could be curious to see other translations of Sites such as Japan, China, France, German, Russian, Korean Ect.

item.title: Question re: Editing others' articles: Moderator Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Triple-bracket links are superior to single-bracket links and should be used where possible, such as cross-links with other pages on the wiki. As such they are considered allowable formatting edits on pages.

However, it is important to be sure that they are used appropriately. Triple-bracket links with a full URL (e.g. [[[http://www.scpwiki.com/some-page]]]) are no better than single-bracket links, and some kinds of references (most notably forums) cannot be referenced using triple-bracket page name notation (i.e. [[[some-page|Link label here]]].

Provided that you are only editing pages you come across casually, I'm fine with these changes. Please do not seek out large numbers of pages to edit. If you have an extensive list of pages to be changed, please bring it to staff instead.

Also note that staff reserves the right to issue cease-and-desist orders for any edits being carried out in an improper manner.

item.title: Question re: Editing others' articles: Question re: Editing others' articles
item.content:encoded:
Hi, I hope I've found the right place for site rules questions. My question's about this rule (emphasis mine):

Editing others' articles: You may correct grammar, spelling, or formatting errors on other peoples' articles. Please put a summary of changes in the 'description of changes' box. Any further edits require permission from the original author or Rewrite team.

Often, an on-site link (triple brackets) is formatted as an external link (single brackets). As the Google Chrome users among you may already know, these external links don't work for some users while they are logged in (because we visit scp-wiki.wikidot.com instead of scpwiki.com as a login workaround; visiting any link formatted as https://scpwiki.com/whatever gives us an error message1). Clicking a link that ought to be an on-site link and receiving an error is unpleasant, as is manually replacing scpwiki with scp-wiki.wikidot in the address bar.

I don't know if using the 'wrong' link type is technically a formatting error, but I am hoping to receive mod/admin confirmation that it falls under this category.2 Changing these links' format type would not alter the article text, it would just make the links work properly for those of us who have to use scp-wiki.wikidot.com instead of scpwiki.com for technical reasons.

Getting author permission each and every time, just to change link formatting type from external to on-site, would be hugely annoying for both the editor and the author. I am hoping that we readers can be allowed to make these formatting changes without obtaining author permission each time.

So, tl;dr: If a link is formatted as an external link, but would work properly as an on-site link, may we go ahead and make that formatting change without bothering the author for their permission?

Footnotes
1. Note that the error only appears when an external link specifies https. It's some sort of certificate recognition error. Links formatted as on-site links work fine for us Chrome users, because wikidot helpfully inserts whichever base address the reader is already using.
2. I actually created an account because I wanted to fix various minor errors as I came across them while reading.

item.title: Could you swap the locations of 'delete' and 'link'?: Re: Could you swap the locations of 'delete' and 'link'?
item.content:encoded:
Thread moved from PF/Feature Requests to Proposals and Policy, because it's a CSS change that can be implemented into Sigma-9.

Noting that this request refers to the buttons at the bottom of a forum post.

While there's not much point swapping the buttons, they are for sure way too close together, especially on mobile - they're not touch friendly at all (this article I found recommends at least 12px spacing between text buttons). At minimum there should be more space around them. I'll pass along this request to the rest of the tech team, thank you for bringing it up.

item.title: Could you swap the locations of 'delete' and 'link'?: Could you swap the locations of 'delete' and 'link'?
item.content:encoded:
I am afraid if I'll accidentally press 'delete' rather than 'edit' since they are a bit too close to each other.
So could you please do something about this little problem?

item.title: Tag Thread 3: Revenge of the Tags: Re: Tag Request — kervier
item.content:encoded:
Denied per the wishes of the primary author (djkaktus).

Additionally, most of the uses of this tag are name drops that would not deserve being tagged as such. More substantive uses of the group would be necessary for this proposal to meet the tag requirements.

item.title: Tag Thread 3: Revenge of the Tags: Tag Request — kervier
item.content:encoded:
+ kervier === DENIED
– hide block

Tag request: kervier

A tag for all articles mentioning Kervier International and its subsidiaries. I get that it a niche that's almost exclusive to djkaktus' works, and he has his right to deny this like he did for Dr0Shadow's department-of-abnormalities suggestion above.

Article Title Author Mention
SCP-2610 Procreation by djkaktus Kervier Shipping Company
SCP-2844 Gary of the Paperclips by djkaktus Kervier Mining Company
SCP-2951 10,000 Years by djkaktus Kervier International, "Kervier"1
SCP-3060 Sleep Paralysis by Jacob_Conwell Kervier Logistics, Kervier Pharmaceutical
SCP-3355 St. Nick by djkaktus Kervier Intelligent Systems Company
SCP-3813 The Fall of Hyperion by djkaktus, Doctor Cimmerian, VolgunStrife, and Zhange. Kervier International
SCP-5935 Blood and the Breaking of My Heart by djkaktus "Michelangelo Kervier"2
djkaktus's Proposal III (Offset 1) Summa Modus Operandi by djkaktus Kervier International
djkaktus's Proposal III (Fragment 14) The Seventh by djkaktus "Kervier"3
Footnotes
1. Doesn't explicitly say "Kervier Mining Company", but it crosslinks to SCP-2844.
2. Name mentioned once; possible founder?
3. International in general?

item.title: Tag Thread 3: Revenge of the Tags: Re: Tag Request — the-nameless
item.content:encoded: Tag approved as nameless. The "the-" prefix was removed for consistency with other tags.

item.title: Collapsable side panel on all browser sizes: Re: Collapsable side panel on all browser sizes
item.content:encoded: Those are some very reasonable arguments, and I can't help but agree. It's a shame about the sidebar collapsing, but I totally understand. Thanks for listening, and putting in the work to fix this :)

item.title: Tag Thread 3: Revenge of the Tags: Re: Tag Request — the-nameless
item.content:encoded: The new SCP-5925 can also be added to that list.

item.title: Tag Thread 3: Revenge of the Tags: Tag Request — the-nameless
item.content:encoded:
+ nameless == APPROVED
– hide block

Tag request: the-nameless

A tag for all articles concerning SCP-4000's interpretation of the faefolk. I've consulted the request with Peppersghost themselves and they said they said they are fine with both the request and the name of the tag.

Articles:

  • SCP-4000 by PeppersGhost
  • SCP-4000-J by djkaktus
  • A walk in the land of the nameless. by Uncle Nicolini
  • SCP-5404 by PeppersGhost
  • The Grove of Exiles by TechSorcerer2747
  • SCP-5935 by djkaktus
  • A Fellow Scholar by DrChandra
  • Another djkaktus Proposal by djkaktus
  • How Is Your Name? by DrChandra
  • The Great Shucks of the Unnamed Lords by DrChandra
  • Old Business by MaliceAforethought
  • The Knight of Flowers by Cerastes
  • SCP-5242 by Placeholder McD
  • SCP-5925 by TheSlothSavant

13 articles by 7 authors.

item.title: Collapsable side panel on all browser sizes: Re: Collapsable side panel on all browser sizes
item.content:encoded:
Moved from PF/Feature Requests to Proposals and Policy, because it's a request specific to Sigma-9 and not any of Wikidot's CSS (I checked and there are no media queries for screen width in either the base Wikidot theme or the original Shiny theme, which Sigma-9 is forked from). The changes you want to make are possible right now.

This is a long post, so I've collapsed parts of it.

+ A case for increasing the breakpoint width
– hide block

I'm finding myself agreeing with the spirit of this request. There's this awkward, often-ignored breakpoint between 768px and about 900px where the screen is too thin to accomodate a full width of content and also the sidebar, but the sidebar is crammed in there anyway. It's gotten to the point where some CSS themes have started using a hybrid breakpoint to select widths where the page content is reduced, but even then it's a subpar experience for users at those resolutions.

The page content is supposed to be 700px wide. The width of the sidebar is 11em, which at a font size of 0.8em and assuming default browser font settings is 140.8px, so the absolute minimum breakpoint for most users to ensure that the width of the content is never reduced because of the sidebar is 840.8px. With variation in default font size between users and changes in root font size between themes, I'd probably bump that up to the more standard 1024px, just to be safe. I do think your suggestion of 1500px is too high - at that point you're not gaining any reading width, just losing the sidebar for the sake of it.

It'd be easy to change the breakpoint in Sigma-9, but that's not the only thing that would need to change. We have more than 80 CSS themes that would need to be updated, and that's just on this wiki - I'm not personally sure which if any of the international SCP branches are reliant on EN's Sigma-9, and there's also a host of sandboxes, private sandboxes, SCP offshoots and other sites that use Sigma-9 or a derivative of it. EN has made many changes to our copy of Sigma-9, but nothing as breaking as this. It would be a lot of effort to make sure nothing breaks.

We are a writing site, though, and the most important part of writing is not writing but reading. I think it's important to make sure the reading experience is, if not as good as possible, then at least not unnecessarily-ruined. I think the effort is worth it.

Noting that we have a PR to Sigma-9 to effect the breakpoint change: https://github.com/scpwiki/sigma9/pull/40

+ A case against making the sidebar collapsible on desktop
– hide block

Making the sidebar collapsible on all screen sizes is a bit more iffy. The way it's implemented in Sigma-9 currently is a bit of a hack using the CSS :target selector, which selects the element specified by the URL - it's where the "#side-bar" in the URL comes from when you open the side bar. As a result there's a whole bunch of weird side effects - it gets added to browser history so closing the sidebar and going Back will open it again, bookmarking a page with the sidebar open will have the sidebar open when you come back to it, and there's no way to remember whether or not the sidebar was open when navigating between pages. Retaining a desktop/tablet user's preference for whether or not the sidebar is open is just not possible. It would be a useless and annoying feature, having to close the sidebar every time you went to a new page. I'd give up on it pretty quickly.

Noting that we have a Project Foundation issue to create a native sidebar that doesn't rely on hacks: https://scuttle.atlassian.net/browse/WJ-312

To sum up: while making the sidebar always collapsible isn't useful, increasing the breakpoint width is, and I think it would be worth the effort to do so.

item.title: Collapsable side panel on all browser sizes: Collapsable side panel on all browser sizes
item.content:encoded:
Description: Wikidot uses adaptive scaling for its UI elements. There is a navigation panel on the left side of the site that, when the screen is less than 768 pixels1, collapses off screen with a button to bring it in and out. I request that this panel have be collapsible regardless of window size (although it would be open by default for medium-large screens). An example of this behavior can be found here.

If this is not possible, a simpler option would be to change the 768 breakpoint to a larger value. For this I conjecture that 1500 pixels would be approximately correct.

Reason: The side panel does not adapt well to tablet devices. When using a tablet device in portrait mode, the panel is stuck taking up about a third of the screen, while providing little value. This, in my opinion, dramatically harms the reading experience. This problem is easily remedied for all devices by making the panel collapsible.

In the event that this is not feasible, I suggest a breakpoint of 1500 pixels based on my knowledge of common device sizes. Once you step down from a desktop monitor, devices are almost exclusively tablets and smartphones2. On either of those devices, the panel should be hidden by default, regardless of their horizontal resolution. For this reason, I suggest a value that is only a little lower than the standard horizontal resolution on a modern device, while still allowing for some padding for DPI scaled devices, such as laptops. This solution has weird edge cases, and could easily cause problems for other device sizes, so I don't recommend it unless there is no better option.

Advantages:

  • Improved readability for tablet devices, and other large portrait screens (some desktop setups use a "sideways" monitor for reading).
  • More economical use of screen space

Disadvantages:

  • Slight increase in UI "clutter", as a new button would need to be added
  • Increases required clicks by 1 for some navigation tasks on some browsers
Footnotes
1. This is the breakpoint listed in the CSS. In practice, the value will change based on various scaling settings of the device, because nothing is ever simple.
2. When considering actual hardware resolution. Laptops considerably complicate this problem because they frequently use DPI scaling, making them appear lower resolution than they actually are. My 1080p laptop "looks" like it has a horizontal resolution of 1650 pixels, because of DPI scaling.

item.title: Accessible Issue: Re: Accessible Issue
item.content:encoded: You're welcome! For some information on co-authoring, see this page: http://www.scpwiki.com/zyns-co-authoring-tips

item.title: Accessible Issue: Re: Accessible Issue
item.content:encoded: I’ll try these before making anymore suggestions, thanks!

item.title: Accessible Issue: Re: Accessible Issue
item.content:encoded:
A forum where people can post short drafts for fun,

The Fan Work forum is for posting "for fun" drafts.

and maybe through a system if one becomes popular enough it could be promoted to a full SCP.

allows new people to easily contribute to the site.

The SCP wiki holds all authors to the same standards, whether brand new or veterans. Having an "easy version" way to fast-track articles to the mainlist with looser requirements runs the risk of encouraging new authors to not bother to develop the skills necessary to write quality material.

an alternative, possibly less stressful method to get concepts out

Have you considered asking an experienced writer to co-author with you?

item.title: Accessible Issue: Re: Accessible Issue
item.content:encoded:
Oh I totally understand the stress on critters, I wouldn't want to add more. Furthermore too clarify two things:
1: I still think its reasonable to say some pages could reach deletion criteria before finding its audience.
2: I do actually agree with the purpose of the high expectations. Even without a successful SCP I've learned valuable things here and appreciate the work.

If you were asking me for suggestions, I think the main thing I'd bring to the table is a more carefree wing of the site. You have the main foundation, the -J's, perhaps a 'Burner Foundation' could be useful? A training ground if you will. A forum where people can post short drafts for fun, and maybe through a system if one becomes popular enough it could be promoted to a full SCP. This is just spitballing but I hope you see what I'm getting at, an alternative, possibly less stressful method to get concepts out. Could have a word maximum, wouldn't need to give critiquers extra work, and allows new people to easily contribute to the site.

item.title: Accessible Issue: Re: Accessible Issue
item.content:encoded:
Hey ResearcherRando, thanks for bringing these issues up. If we could get some clarification on one of your points, that'd be great.

+ Snipped for mobile users
– hide block

I think the ability for more freedom in trying to convey what makes an idea worth it could benefit this site greatly. Once again: this spot isn’t impossible, but cam present real problems to someone in trying to cram an idea into a small format.

What are you suggesting we change, specifically? That we raise the word count limit on idea posts? Authors have a number of resources at their disposal already to receive crit which aren't encumbered by a word limit, whether that's the IRC chatrooms, a couple trusted Discord servers, or other avenues.1 Moreover the greenlighting process isn't mandatory for posting to the site — it's only a requirement in order to use the draft forum.

With that said, authors seems pretty free to mix and match what styles of critique work for them.

For a site requiring hard work from new creators, all it takes is ten people deciding to press a button saying they didn’t like it, and that says to the author this page is worthy of deletion.

Bit of a correction here: the -10 deletion threshold means that ten more people downvote the page than upvote it, not that ten people overall downvote.

in trying to add to something I’ve seen as fun and a little silly, I’ve been met with an incredibly high wall, in some ways earned by the site, but in many ways just as ridiculous.

I agree, it takes a lot of work to get something to stick on this site. If you're driven to see your idea(s) succeed, we encourage you to continue working on them up-until-and-beyond the point that they'll stay on the site. This site's high standards are meant as less of an impassible boundary and more as a motivator.

Footnotes
1. The word limit was initially put in place at the request of critters — before it was implemented, ideas tended to be exceedingly long and directionless. The rule intends to force new authors to streamline their ideas and make them look more appealing to critters (moreso than giant walls of text, anyways). Personally I think 400 words is rather generous.

item.title: Accessible Issue: Accessible Issue
item.content:encoded:
Hello,

So the SCP Foundation is an incredible premise that can create some unique stories based on it’s premise alone. That being said, as someone who has now made multiple attempts at SCP’s I’d like to talk about some issues I see in the sight as an outsider.

+ Greenlight Troubles
- Greenlight Troubles

The site’s process for creating new SCPs, especially for new creators, is close focused at best. The fictional Foundation is supposed to be a clinical world facing fantastical elements, but as someone coming in, the similarities to the real site are a bit unnerving. To say nothing of the users, from personal experience they are fine, and sometimes friendly, this is purely about how a brand new author. To give credit, yes an author can develop ideas outside the site plenty, but that doesn't change they then have to convince at least two reviewers in a small paragraph there idea is worth it. Not impossible, obviously, but a little convoluted. I think the ability for more freedom in trying to convey what makes an idea worth it could benefit this site greatly. Once again: this spot isn’t impossible, but cam present real problems to someone in trying to cram an idea into a small format.

+ Aaaand its gone
- Aaaand its gone

The rating system means with -10 likes, a page meets the criteria for deletion. Now again, I can appreciate the benefits of running a strict site, but something about this seems a little cruel. For a site requiring hard work from new creators, all it takes is ten people deciding to press a button saying they didn’t like it, and that says to the author this page is worthy of deletion. It could be absolutely certain that if every site had a shallow enough criteria, many beloved posts would be gone before they blew up. Im not saying enough downvotes isn’t indicative of a post possibly needing deletion, but maybe that particular field needs to be stretched, and authors in general given a chance. After all theres no way to actually know if the majority of site users dislike an SCP, the votes are only indicative of the people who found that page.

+ Slightly Personal
- slightly personal

Once again, I’m sure to some this seems like the ravings of a writer who was a little hurt, and in some part I give credit that it does come from that. More so though, I’d argue that this is also the perspective of someone who has worked hard to try and join really creative people, and been turned away not necessarily by them, but by a system coming across as super insular. It occurs to me maybe I’m also ridiculous for trying to join a catalogue cresting the 6000’s, but under the impression that this universe could be even more I did wanna try. Definitely some fault is mine in writing, lets not pretend any old SCP’s I made would be the next 096, but my point lies in that: in trying to add to something I’ve seen as fun and a little silly, I’ve been met with an incredibly high wall, in some ways earned by the site, but in many ways just as ridiculous.

In closing: the site’s a little too insular. This doesn’t necessarily warrant a whole overhaul, but maybe a reflective look at how some things should be run. I suppose if you want to write this off as a newbee venting, thats fine, but I think it would great benefit the Foundation to genuinely look at how it handles new ideas, and what kind of criteria it has, and whether or not its always fair.
